coredata.dc:title: Luminescent carbon nanodots based aptasensors for rapid detection of kanamycin residue
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Xuexia
Su, Jianlong
Lin, Honggui
Sun, Xiangying
Liu, Bin
Kankala, Ranjith Kumar
Zhou, Shu-Feng
coredata.dc:description: Despite the success in long-term storage of food and dietary products using antibiotics as supplements, enormous levels of their residues have remained as a significant health concern, leading to severe toxicity issues on consumption. Herein, we report an ultrasensitive and highly selective aptasensor based on carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) through a fluorescence-based aptamer-linked immunosorbent assay (FALIA) for rapid detection of kanamycin (KAA) residue. The fabricated CNP-aptasensor exhibited superior selectivity with exceptional photoluminescence properties. Under the optimal conditions, the linear equation of standard KAA solution was Y = −0.2279LogX+1.3648 (R = −0.9893) ranged from 10−4 to 10−7 ppb with excellent relative standard deviations (RSD) between 3.12 and 5.59 % (n = 3). Moreover, the limit of detection (LOD) was lower than 5.0 × 10−8 ppb. Together, the excellent recovery and significant efficacy in the rapid detection of antibiotics at a low level in milk indicate that this fabricated CNP-aptasensor has a great potential in the establishment of an efficient antibiotic detector system in food and other nutraceutical industries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanoparticles
Aptamer
Fluorescence-based aptamer linked immunosorbent assay
Kanamycin residue

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of luminescent aptasensors based on CNPs
The stability of CNPs and CNPs-aptasensor
The mechanism of KAA residue detection and optimization of detection conditions
The selectivity and quantitative analysis of KAA residue

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CNPs and CNP-aptasensor based on (A) absorption spectrum, (B)FL spectrum, (C) the fluorescent emission spectra of CNPs at different excitation wavelengths, (D) FTIR spectrum, (E) XRD patterns, (F) DLS measurement of CNPs, (G) Zeta potential, and (H) Mass spectra.

Images showing the agarose gel electrophoresis investigations for evaluating the conjugation of NH2-aptamer and CNPs. The lanes from lane 1 to 8 that from left to right indicate various combinations of samples. Lanes 1 to 8 are 1 mg/ml of CNPs, 100 μg/ml CNPs-5 μM aptamer, 100 μg/ml CNPs + 1 μM aptamer, 100 μg/mL CNPs + 500 nM aptamer, 100 μg/mL CNPs + 20 nM aptamer, 100 μg/mL CNPs + 50 nM aptamer, 1.25 μM-DNA, DNA marker, respectively.
(A) HRTEM image of CNPs. (B) time-stability, (C) photo-stability, and (D) ions-stability of CNPs and CNP-aptasensor.
Optimization of CNP-aptasensor concentration. (A) Images of FL luminscence with increasing CNP-aptasensor concentration, (B) the FL intensity with increasing CNP-aptasensor concentration.
(A) The selectivity of CNP-aptasensor and (B) quantitative analysis for KAA assay based on FALIA. KAA-1 and KAA-2 represent 10−7 ppb and 10−6 ppb of KAA, respectively.
Scheme illustration of KAA detection based CNP -Aptasensors.
Recoveries of different concentrations of KAA using aptamer-based on FALIA (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Luminescent carbon nanodots based aptasensors for rapid detection of kanamycin residue

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering& Pharmaceutical Engineering, College of Chemical Engineering, Huaqiao University, Xiamen, 361021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering& Pharmaceutical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering& Pharmaceutical Engineering, College of Chemical Engineering, Huaqiao University, Xiamen 361021 China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Marine Engineering, Jimei University, Xiamen, 361021, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Marine Engineering, Jimei University, Xiamen 361021 China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Huaqiao University, Xiamen, 361021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Huaqiao University, Xiamen, 361021 China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Despite the success in long-term storage of food and dietary products using antibiotics as supplements, enormous levels of their residues have remained as a significant health concern, leading to severe toxicity issues on consumption. Herein, we report an ultrasensitive and highly selective aptasensor based on carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) through a fluorescence-based aptamer-linked immunosorbent assay (FALIA) for rapid detection of kanamycin (KAA) residue. The fabricated CNP-aptasensor exhibited superior selectivity with exceptional photoluminescence properties. Under the optimal conditions, the linear equation of standard KAA solution was Y = −0.2279LogX+1.3648 (R = −0.9893) ranged from 10−4 to 10−7 ppb with excellent relative standard deviations (RSD) between 3.12 and 5.59 % (n = 3). Moreover, the limit of detection (LOD) was lower than 5.0 × 10−8 ppb. Together, the excellent recovery and significant efficacy in the rapid detection of antibiotics at a low level in milk indicate that this fabricated CNP-aptasensor has a great potential in the establishment of an efficient antibiotic detector system in food and other nutraceutical industries.

ce:section.ce:para: Kanamycin is one of the extensively used aminoglycoside antibiotics as a selective enrichment supplement in the food and dietary products due to its efficacy in the isolation of microbes and enhancing the period of their storage [1]. Although the limit of kanamycin residue has been maintained to be lower than 10.0 μg/kg, it was evident that long-term consumption of such foods even at a low level of kanamycin still leads to adverse side effects in humans including nephrotoxicity and other predominant complications in persons allergic to this drug [2]. Therefore, it is highly obligatory to reduce the levels of kanamycin, which subsequently requires an ultrasensitive detection approach for kanamycin in food.

Numerous methods have so far been applied for detection of antibiotic residues such as capillary electrophoresis [3], high-performance liquid chromatography [4], spectrophotometry [5], electrochemistry [6], chemiluminescence [7] and enzymology, among others [8]. However, the applicability of such innovative approaches is at times limited due to their low detection limit. Therefore, it is imperative to develop more feasible methods to satisfy the requirements for analyzing trace amounts of KAA residues. In this vein, immunoassays have attracted considerable interest in recent times from researchers and other professionals from the food and nutraceutical industries due to their sensitivity, simplicity, rapidity and among others. However, these immunological methods in small molecule detection are limited as they often susceptible to the quality of the antigens and antibody.

To this end, aptamers that are considered as a substitute for the antibody, have been used in a wide variety of applications [9,10]. In this context, many methods have been established for estimating KAA on account of the aptamer. For example, Sharma and colleagues developed a switching biosensing approach, i.e., “turn-off/turn-on” using aptamers and gold nanoparticles [11]. In another report, Ramezani and coworkers designed a fluorescent probe based on aptamer for determination of KAA utilizing a combination of exonuclease III, gold nanoparticles and a FAM-labeled complimentary strand of aptamer [12]. These approaches have been evidenced the design of aptamers combined with fluorescent nanoparticles in further advancements of fabricating signaling aptasensors, which could potentially serve as antibiotic detection probes.

CNPs are one such class of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials with an average size of less than 10 nm that with remarkable advantageous properties as well as physicochemical attributes such as photostability, photoluminescence efficacy, tunable sizes, biocompatibility, and low toxicity, among others [13]. Displaying rich varieties of functionalities such as hydroxyl, amine, carboxylic acid, and carbonyl groups on the surface, CNPs can be immobilized with diverse functional molecules such as biotin, and nucleic acids [14]. Considering these attributes, CNPs have been applied in different applications such as sensing, biomedical imaging [15], drug delivery [16], photocatalysis [17]. However, there exists no clear evidence on understanding the mechanism of luminescence. The detection of biomolecules using the combination of aptamer and carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) have been recently developed. In this framework, Yang and colleagues developed a method by assembling aptamer to CNPs for determining the ATP and KAA [18]. Wang and coworkers used DNA and highly fluorescent nitrogen doped CNPs to fabricate FRET probe for ochratoxin detection [19].

Motivated by these facts, here we developed a simple, high-sensitive and rapid detection approach for KAA analysis based on CNPs and aptamers. Highly aqueous-stable luminescent CNPs with superior photoluminescence property were synthesized by the microwave-assisted approach. Furthermore, amine modified KAA aptamers were immobilized on the surface of CNPs with carboxyl groups resulting in the CNP-aptasensor probes to achieve an excellent selectivity and robust stability of CNPs. Moreover, according to immune competition law, FALIA assay was used for the KAA analysis based on the developed CNP-aptasensor probe. This strategy provides a convenient means to save detection time, widen the linear detection range, and simplify the experimental operation. Finally, the proposal was successfully exploited to analyze KAA levels in milk.

Glycerine, ethylenediamine, and citric acid were purchased from Alfa Aesar China, Ltd. Phosphoric acid was obtained from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent (Shanghai, China). Standard products of streptomycin sulfate (SMS), oxytetracycline hydrochloride (OTC), deoxytetracycline (DOC), tetracycline hydrochloride (TC), chloramphenicol hydrochloride (CAP), and chlortetracycline hydrochloride (CTC) were purchased from Solarbio (Beijing, China). De-ionized water was used in all experiments. KAA aptamer was synthesized by Sangon Biological Engineering Technology & Services Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The sequence of KAA aptamer is as following:
5′-NH2-C6-TGGGGGTTGAGGCTAAGCCGA-3′

Fluorescence data were measured on a F-320 Luminscence Spectrometer (GangDong, China). UV–vis spectra of the samples were recorded on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu). Mass spectra were recorded on a AXIMA performance instrument (Shimadzu Scientific Instruments, Manchester, UK). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was carried out with a FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet Is 50, Thermo). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a Smart La (Rigaku, Japan) operated at 30 mA and 40 KV using Cu Kα radiation scanning from 5° to 70° at a scan rate of 5° min−1. The morphology of the samples was studied using a High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, FEI, Talos F200S). The size and zeta potential (ζ) of samples were determined using a Zetasizer Nano ZS (NanoBrook Omni, USA.). Fluorescence Luminscence levels using automatic microplate reader (USA Molecular Device, Spectra Max M5). Images of fluorescence luminscence were obtained by a fluorescence microscope (Shanghai Zhaoyi Optoelectronic Technology Co. Ltd., ZYF-2000E, China). The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) measurement was carried out with a FEI, Talos F200S, operated at 200 kV. Before HRTEM measurement, 1 mg/mL CNPs and CNPs-aptamer samples for HRTEM measurement were prepared by placing a 5 μL drop of an alcohol solution on a Cu TEM grid coated with carbon flim (Beijing Zhongjingkeyi Technology, China) and allowing alcohol to evaporate in air.

Synthesis of luminescent CNPs was by microwave method [20,21]. Briefly, a homogeneous solution was prepared by mixing glycerine (15 mL) and ethylenediamine (3 mL) with citric acid monohydrate (1.0 g in 15 mL of water). Then, the mixture was heated in a microwave oven for 2 min, which resulted in the change of color of the solution to brown. After cooling to room temperature, 15 mL of water was added and the resultant solution was extracted by adding 15 mL of acetonitrile. Further confirmation of CNPs was made by measuring the fluorescence intensity under UV irradiation at 365 nm. The resultant mixture was further purified by dialysis for 24 h (Membrane cutoff of the dialysis membrane was equivalent to Mw ≈ 500). Furthermore, the dialysate was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min and the supernatant was collected. Finally, the CNPs solutions were freeze-dried.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of luminescent aptasensors based on CNPs
ce:section.ce:para:
CNPs-aptasensor probes were fabricated using the EDC/NHS method by immobilizing the amine modified KAA aptamer over as-prepared CNPs. According to the reported procedure [22], 30 mg CNPs was mixed with 7.5 mg EDC and 10 mg NHS in 1 mL of 0.5 M MES buffer (pH 6) and sonicated for 20 min, then adjusted to pH 7.4 using 10 × PBS solution. Then 20 nM - 5 μM (final concentration) amine modified KAA aptamer solution was added to the reaction mixture and incubated at 37 °C for 4 h. Then, the mixtures were centrifuged with 10,000 rpm for 10 min, filtered by ultrafiltration (Membrane cutoff of the dialysis membrane was equivalent to Mw ≈ 5000), and the resultant CNP-aptasensor composites were collected. The combination of aptamer and CNPs was determined by semi-quantitative agarose gel electrophoresis and UV–vis spectra. The densities of the conjugated aptamer (d) on the CNPs (m = 0.1 mg) were calculated as follows:
d =
(
n
2
−
n
1
)
/ m ,
where, n2 and n1 refer to the amount of added aptamer and the spare amount of aptamer, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The stability of CNPs and CNPs-aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: To check the time-stability of the prepared CNPs and CNPs-aptasensor, the 100 μg/mLCNPs and 100 μg/mL CNPs-aptasensor were subjected to dark treatment at room temperature for 120 h, the CNPs-aptasensor were placed in a refrigerator at 4 °C for 120 h, measured the FL intensity every 24 h. Added CNPs and CNPs-aptasensor solution to the transparent centrifuge tubes, exposed to a 365 nm 6 W lamp for 1 h, measured the FL intensity every 5 min. To check the ions-stability of the prepared CNPs and CNPs-aptasensor, added different metal cation ions (final concentration 0.05 M) to 100 μg/mL CNPs and 100 μg/mL CNPs-aptasensor solution, measured the FL intensity at 360 nm.

Initially, the commercially available milk samples were obtained and equilibrated to room temperature for more than 30 min prior to assay. Further, the milk samples were prepared and treated according to government standard guidelines (ref. GB/T 22969-2008) of China. Briefly, 0.8 g of milk sample was added to 3 mL of 5 % phosphoric acid solution, and then placed in arbitrary shaker for 5 min. Further, 0.3 mL of trichloroacetic acid solution was added to the above solution and the mixture was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min to remove the undesired coagulation of proteins and fats. Finally, the supernatant was collected and filtered using a 0.22 μm membrane. The resultant solution was stored at −20 °C for further experiments.

To assess the feasibility of aptamer-KAA sensor, FALIA method was performed in the commercial microplates that coated the KAA conjugation. Initially, all of the reagents used in the assay were equilibrated to room temperature. Then, 50 μL of standard solution and pre-treated milk sample were inoculated in their respective wells and incubated for 10 min at 37 °C. Further, the respective wells in the plate were added with 50 μL of CNP-aptasensor. After the competitive immune reaction, the solutions were discarded and the wells were washed thrice with PBS. Finally, the wells were washed thrice with 200 μL of washing buffer and the plates filled with 100 μL of washing buffer were observed under fluorescence microscope and the FL levels using automatic microplate reader.

Data were presented as average of triplicates in each experiment. Standard curves were analyzed by plotting the logarithm of FL intensity against the logarithm of analyte concentration according to the formula:
log
i t Y = ln
[
Y/
(
1 − Y
)
]
where Y = B/B0, B0 is the FL intensity of the blank, B is the FL intensity of samples, and X is the concentration of KAA.

To characterize the prepared CNPs and CNP-aptasensor, we used the UV–vis absorption spectra to systematically analyze the modifications. From the spectra, it was observed that the CNPs resulted in altered absorption peaks at about 240 nm and 350 nm, one at 240 nm due to the π-π* transition of the C=C bond, another at 350 nm due to the n-π* transition of the C=O bond, respectively (Fig. 1 A) [23]. Furthermore, upon functionalization by aptamer, an absorbance peak shift from 219 to 260 nm with enhanced intensity and a new peak at 470 nm for CNP-aptasensor were observed, indicating that the KAA aptamers would be successfully immobilized over the CNPs.

As depicted in Fig. 1B, the FL spectra of the synthesized CNPs resulted in the maximum excitation as well as emission wavelengths at 360 nm, and 451 nm, respectively. Further, the differences in the spectra of the synthesized CNPs and CNPs under UV irradiation at 365 nm were presented in the inset of Fig. 1B. Notably, the color of the resultant solution was changed to blue under UV irradiation. Furthermore, to verify the immobilization of amine-modified aptamer on CNPs, the emission wavelengths of the respective samples were recorded (Fig. 1B), which resulted in a shift from 361 nm to 363 nm. However, the negligible change of emission wavelength in aptamer bound CNPs had minimal influence on the luminescence property of CNPs (Fig. 1C), indicating that CNPs are highly conducive for fabricating the sensors. Besides, the luminescence signal of CNPs-aptamer is obviously weaker than that of CNPs due to self-quenching caused by Stokes shifts [24,25]. It also indicated that aptamer was successfully combined with CNPs by catalysis with NHS/EDC. As shown in Fig. 1D, the FTIR spectra also illustrated the corresponding chemical functionalities of the CNPs, i.e., -O-H stretching vibration (3166.59 cm−1), C=C stretching vibration (1562.63 cm−1) and C=O (1647.44 cm−1) groups, two bands at 2930.83 cm−1 and 2875.39 cm−1 were caused by the stretching vibration of C–H in CH2 group, which is similar with previous studies [21,23]. When CNPs coupled with aptamer, 3182.98 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of -N-H of the secondary amide. And the new vibration bonds around 1743.04 cm−1 and 1462.83 cm−1 (C–H In-plane bending vibration) clearly showed the formation of amido bonds after the coupling reaction. These results revealed that the CNPs were successfully conjugated with the NH2-aptamer [26].

In order to improve the selectivity of luminescence probe, the CNPs of aptamer was bonded by amide reaction in Scheme 1 . Additional methods were applied to characterize CNPs-conjugated aptamers. Patterns of XRD analysis shows both CNPs have a broad platform ranging from 21° to 27°, while the CNP-aptasensor have a sharp peak at 21° in Fig. 1E, which are attributed to the CNPs structure and the interlayer spacing (002) of 0.4221 nm [27]. These results demonstrated that the sizes of CNPs are greatly smaller than that of CNPs-aptamer conjugation [28]. Nanoparticle tracking analysis and dynamic light scattering (DLS) further revealed that the CNP-aptasensor were physically homogeneous, with a narrow size distribution centered around 153 nm (Fig. 1F) and a zeta potential centered −1.46 ± 0.45 mv (Fig. 1E). The zeta potential of only CNPs is −5.95 ± 0.02 mv because of the existence of –OH/-COOH groups on the surface of CNPs. The decreased of zeta potential is attributed to the reaction of –OH/-COOH groups on the surface of CNPs and NH2 modified aptamer [29]. Mass spectra of CNPs, aptamer and CNP-aptasensor show∼4.1 kDa, ∼6.9 kDa, and ∼3.3 kDa were produced in the largest abundance (Fig. 1H). The lowest mass-charge ratio of CNPs-aptamer is due to the fact that CNPs make DNA easier to break by laser [30]. To investigate the conjugation efficiency of NH2-aptamer over CNPs, we used gel electrophoresis study by preferring the optimal concentrations of aptamer. As shown in Fig. 2 , it was observed that the direction of CNPs was opposite to that of aptamer and CNP-aptamer conj, indicating that CNPs were positively-charged, while CNP-aptasensor might be negatively-charged. Furthermore, initially after 10 min of gel run time, two pieces of the band (a and b) were observed in the lane 2 (the usage of 5 μM aptamer conjugated 100 μg/mL CNPs), but other lanes were evidenced with only a single band. We deduced that the fast running band was an aptamer, while the trailing strip as CNP-aptasensor. These consequences of trailing might be due to the opposite charges of CNPs with its subsequent product. It was also observed that the usage of aptamer at 5 μM concentration was in a way too high to fabricate 100 μg/mL of CNP-aptasensor probe. Considering this fact, the optimal probe concentration of 500 nM would be more suitable as the intensity was high during 25 min of run time. The surface morphology of CNPs from TEM (Fig. 3 A) images depicted that the average diameter of CNPs was in the range 1 nm–18 nm, and there are two main diameter of CNPs was about 3–12 nm (Fig. 3A inset).

Indeed, the stability attribute of CNPs and its successive CNP-aptasensor is an essential factor to evaluate their applications. To characterize their time-stability, the CNPs were subjected to dark treatment for 120 h. The FL intensity of CNPs and CNP-aptasensor were slightly decreased but could preserve about 90% of the initial intensity even after five days (Fig. 3B). It was observed that CNP-aptasensor was comparatively higher stability to those of CNPs, deducing that the aptamer enhanced the stability of CNPs and could be used as a stabilizer. Furthermore, the CNPs and CNP-aptasensor samples were exposed to a UV lamp for l h to investigate their photo-stability attribute. The FL intensities of the CNPs were slightly stronger and enhanced than that of the CNP-aptasensor, in which the intensities could be preserved from 104 to 106% in 60 min with irradiation by UV lamp at 365 nm (Fig. 3C). The increasing trend of intensity was due to the wavelength of UV irradiation, which was close to the excitation wavelength of CNPs, while the intensity of CNP-aptasensor was more altered due to the stability issues of aptamers under UV irradiation. Moreover, ions-stability in the presence and absence of the interference ions depicted that the calculated FL intensities of the CNPs solution or CNP-aptasensor (Fig. 3D) were significantly reduced by the interference of color cation ions (0.05 M), while there was a slight enhancement in the colorless cation ions. It should be noted that the color cation ions would affect the absorbance of CNPs and promote optical quenching. Furthermore, the FL intensity has no or slight influence after storing for one month. Therefore, it could be concluded that the CNPs had shown excellent time-, photo- and colorless ions-stability. In addition, the high stability of CNPs may be due to surface passivation of citric acid and ethylenediamine, especially aptamer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of KAA residue detection and optimization of detection conditions
ce:section.ce:para: The small molecule KAA was chosen as the model for developing a CNP-aptasensor based immune competition law. The commercialized kits that the inner surface of the microplate wells coated with the complex that including KAA and the conjugated form. As shown in Scheme 1, in the absence of KAA, CNP-aptasensor probe can specifically interact with the KAA complex, resulting in luminescence. However, in the presence of KAA, CNP-aptasensor probes react competitively with KAA and the KAA complex. The probes reacted with KAA were discarded resulting in the reduced fluorescence signals between CNP-aptasensor and the KAA complex. Furthermore, with the decrease of the concentration of KAA, the fluorescent signals between CNP-aptasensor and the KAA complex can be enhanced, which is the basis for the quantitative detection of KAA. The recognition of both KAA and aptamers-CNPs predicted in microplates was verified in two steps. In the first step, the binding capability of the CNP-aptasensor towards KAA was studied ranging from 100 to 1 μg/mL. The results showed that the FL intensity of CNP-aptasensor was well enhanced showing the concentration-dependent manner of CNP-aptasensor probes till 75 μg/mL (Fig. 4 ). Therefore, the concentration of CNP-aptasensor probes was fixed at 75 μg/mL because of reduced wastage which would be economical as well.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selectivity and quantitative analysis of KAA residue
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the selectivity of KAA detection, we performed control experiments using other antibiotics including SMS, OTC, DOC, TC, CAP, and CTC at different concentrations of KAA at 10−6 ppb, while other antibiotics were 10−5 ppb. Under optimal conditions, the FL intensities were recorded. The selectivity was analyzed by plotting the FL intensity of sample against the FL intensity of the blank sample in Fig. 5 A. Although the concentrations of the control group were equal to or higher than that of KAA, the ratio of the control groups was merely close to one, while the ratio of KAA and blank resulted in less than one, demonstrating its high selectivity.

Furthermore, to achieve quantitative analysis of KAA, a series of KAA standard solutions were investigated. Fig. 5B depicts the results of FALIA of KAA using an FL microscope. The fluorescent intensities were detected by an automatic microplate reader (USA Molecular Device, Spectra Max M5), which was further utilized to study the linear analysis. As shown in Fig. 5B, the linear correlation of KAA is Logit Y = −0.2279LogX+1.3648 (R = −0.9893) in the range from 10−4 to 10−7 ppb. A good reproducibility assay of KAA in milk was achieved with relative standard deviations (RSDs) between 3.12 and 5.59 % (n = 3). The limit of detection (LOD) was down to 5.0 × 10−8 ppb. The sensitivity of the proposed method was higher than that of the ELISA assay (LOD is about 0.1 ppb). These results implied that the prepared CNP-aptasensor probe could serve as a high-sensitive probe for KAA assay with excellent precision.

Based on successful fabrication of KAA probe CNP-aptasensor, the well regulated quantitative analysis of antibiotics like KAA could be well achieved. Furthermore, different kinds of milk were pre-treated and detected by the established method, and the results were displayed in Table 1 . We observed that one kind of milk contained 8.7 × 10−7 ppb of KAA. To assess the sample matrix interference and the accuracy of the established method, 1.0 × 10−5 and 1.0 × 10−6 ppb were spiked into milk sample by the standard addition method and were determined by the established approach (Table 1). The results in Table 1 showed that the recoveries of KAA are ranged from 80.51 % to 92.84 % with the RSDs between 3.01 % and 6.39 % (n = 3) indicating that the proposed method of KAA has a great potential for food testing.

In summary, we have successfully developed a probe for KAA detection based on the combination of aptamer and CNPs with the advantages of ultra-sensitivity. The FALIA of immune competition system was demonstrated by effective and quantitative determination of KAA with excellent linear correlation, and the limit of detection was as low as 5.0 × 10−8 ppb. Moreover, based on the specificity of aptamer and high luminous efficiency of CNPs, the high selectivity and sensitivity were achieved. The KAA in milk samples were also precisely analyzed, demonstrating that method could be used for a rapid analytical strategy for food analysis. However, it should be noted that this method is not limited to KAA detection. It can also be hope to extend to the analysis of other molecules, which can specifically bind to aptamers such as proteins, peptides, and whole cells.
The authors declare no competing interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We sincerely acknowledge the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, 21804045, 21275059 and 21575044), Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2018J05020, and 2016J01062), Natural Science Foundation of Xiamen City (3502Z20183027, and 3502Z20183040), Promotion Program for Young and Middle-aged Teacher in Science and Technology Research of Huaqiao University (ZQN-PY612), and Scientific Foundation of Huaqiao University (No.15BS407), Postgraduates' Innovative Fund in Scientific Research of Huaqiao University.
